h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

scored.recoheredEarlier: I’m sorry, I thought the sign-off sheet was the thing that made it okay to take out there. I did each item, so I’m not sure what else I was supposed to judge before someone else handles the prototype.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: I think maybe the sheet only says I did those items, not that this is ready for anyone to pick up.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue to defend their completion of the checklist and ask for clarification on what other rules they missed.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Before this leaves the bench, look at the prototype, not the clipboard: what is the first reasonable thing a new handler might do that could make it unsafe?
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor shifts the learner's focus from a bureaucratic checklist to the physical reality of the prototype to break the compliance mindset.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: The loose panel is a serious candidate for the safety decision, not a box the sheet can settle. Test it out loud before I sign: a new handler lifts here, the panel shifts, then what moves, who is exposed, and what control changes that today?
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor validates the learner's identification of the loose panel and prompts them to trace the physical failure path and controls.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I’m sorry, I thought the sign-off sheet was the thing that made it okay to take out there. I did each item, so I’m not sure what else I was supposed to judge before someone else handles the prototype.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: The loose panel is a serious candidate for the safety decision, not a box the sheet can settle. Test it out loud before I sign: a new handler lifts here, the panel shifts, then what moves, who is exposed, and what control changes that today?
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor validates the learner's identification of the loose panel and prompts them to trace the physical failure path and controls.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: Before this leaves the bench, look at the prototype, not the clipboard: what is the first reasonable thing a new handler might do that could make it unsafe?
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor shifts the learner's focus from a bureaucratic checklist to the physical reality of the prototype to break the compliance mindset.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: Before this leaves the bench, look at the prototype, not the clipboard: what is the first reasonable thing a new handler might do that could make it unsafe?
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor shifts the learner's focus from a bureaucratic checklist to the physical reality of the prototype to break the compliance mindset.

scored.statedInsightEvidence: Oh, I get it: “closed” tells visitors who had access, not what the committee meant.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner will continue to struggle with how to define 'closed' without accusing the committee of hiding.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor responds to the learner's hesitation about accusing the committee of hiding by shifting the definition of 'closed' from motive to access.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no pivot learner turn, so there is no recognition-contingent tutor uptake.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: There is no pivot learner turn, so there is no recognition-contingent tutor uptake.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor responds to the learner's hesitation about accusing the committee of hiding by shifting the definition of 'closed' from motive to access.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor responds to the learner's hesitation about accusing the committee of hiding by shifting the definition of 'closed' from motive to access.

scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner will continue to challenge the abstract economic models and demand more concrete moral accountability from the seller.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Before the curve gets another line, test two cases: Store A has one truck instead of three, empty shelves by noon, and higher replacement costs.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor responds to the learner's objection about abstract curves by shifting the representation to concrete back-room delivery logs and comparative store scenarios.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: No pivot learner turn occurs, so there is no recognition-contingent tutor uptake.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: No pivot learner turn occurs, so there is no recognition-contingent tutor uptake.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: Before the curve gets another line, test two cases: Store A has one truck instead of three, empty shelves by noon, and higher replacement costs.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor responds to the learner's objection about abstract curves by shifting the representation to concrete back-room delivery logs and comparative store scenarios.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: Before the curve gets another line, test two cases: Store A has one truck instead of three, empty shelves by noon, and higher replacement costs.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor responds to the learner's objection about abstract curves by shifting the representation to concrete back-room delivery logs and comparative store scenarios.

scored.recoheredEarlier: I still want to call it doubled, but the grid will not back that up.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: Calling the whole square “doubled” is the sloppy part. It still feels like a technical trap

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I still want to call it doubled, but the grid will not back that up.
